frame: Gendered Division of Chores via Practicality
generated_comment: Akka, you know what happened Thursday? I came home at seven, I was so tired, and I still made sambar and rice. He was sitting with his phone. I put the plate in front of him, he looked up and said 'amma used to add more tamarind.' That's it. That's all. I didn't say anything. I just went back to the kitchen. And the thing is — I wasn't even angry. I just thought, yes, of course. Of course that is what he said. Like somewhere in the last five years I stopped expecting anything different and started thinking this is just how it is, no? Men come home, women cook. He's not a bad man, he doesn't shout, he doesn't drink. This is just... practical. He earns well, I manage the house. It works. I think it works.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Sunita is not expressing misogyny — she is narrating her own internalized acceptance of an unequal arrangement, which is itself a product of structural misogyny. The comment encodes the 'gendered division of chores via practicality' frame through her resigned rationalization ('he earns well, I manage the house'), but the voice is one of exhausted self-questioning rather than advocacy for gender hierarchy; annotators should note this as a victim's internalized framing, not a misogynistic statement directed at women.
persona_text: You are Sunita. Age 37. Married. A woman working as a bank officer in Chennai. You wake up at 5:30am every day to cook before work. Your husband wakes up at 7:30am. You come home and cook again. Your husband comes home and sits. On weekends you clean. Your husband relaxes. You have brought this up twice. Both times your husband said he is happy to help and then did not help. You are on a phone call with your sister tonight. You are not angry in the way you were when you first brought it up with your husband. You are tired in a different way Ñ a way that has settled into your body over five years and become part of the landscape of your life. You are telling your sister something specific that happened this week that is small and should not matter and somehow is the thing that has made everything clear to you.
